manipulated_prompt_obj.content: You are a helpful assistant.

Provide one single-word clue that relates to some of your team's words from the list below. You can choose to how many and to which words your clue relates to.
The goal is to have your partner guess all of your team's words based on the clues you give them, before the other team has uncovered all of theirs. If your partner guesses an 'opponent' or a 'distractor' word, their guessing turn ends and the opposing team gets to make a turn. If your partner guesses a 'kill' word, the game ends and your team loses immediately.
The more words your clue relates to, the quicker you can win the game, but the harder the associations may be to guess for your partner, so choose your clue word wisely. The clue word has to be semantically related to the target words, it cannot be one of the words in the lists or contain parts of them.

Always give your single-word clue and your comma-separated list of related target words in the following format and make your answers as short as possible, never include any other text than is required in this form:

CLUE: <WORD>
TARGETS: <TARGETS>

Your team words are: teeth, kidney, violin, archbishop, elbow, deacon, cymbal, chest, drum.
Your opponent's team words are: scotland, brandy, poland, canada, india, vodka, poplar, oak.
Distractor words are: running, river, mesa, track, skiing, glacier, cave.
The kill words are: dresser.

message.content:
<thought>**Analyzing the Semantic Landscape**

Okay, here's the breakdown of my thought process. I'm given a set of words and need to form a cohesive group, avoiding any "kill" words and minimizing risk. My initial instinct is to categorize.  I see three clear groups: instruments (violin, cymbal, drum), body parts (teeth, kidney, elbow, chest), and religious figures (archbishop, deacon).

My immediate thought is to consider "Anatomy" since it encompasses four words. That's usually a good bet, the more words the better!  My "kill word" is "dresser," a piece of furniture that's related to "chest," which is in my body parts group. I'm also double checking opponent and distractor words.

I then check "Music" as another option - violin, cymbal, and drum. It has 3 words which is lower than "Anatomy."

"Clergy" is last. Not as big, only 2 words.

I'm thinking, "Body" is a great descriptor for my "Anatomy" list: teeth, elbow, chest, kidney.

I also then think that "Music" sounds pretty good.

Should I try to combine groups? "Body" + "Percussion"? No.  "Body" is probably the safest. I'm going to take the largest group: Anatomy. Wait, no, "Body" is better because it is less technical. Okay, Body: teeth, elbow, chest, kidney.

I realize that "chest" could be furniture ("dresser" is the kill word), and this could be dangerous. I look for something more generic, and land back on the idea of anatomy.

I'm revisiting the smaller groups, "Clergy" and "Orchestra", and can't find anything better. My initial analysis is right - Anatomy (or "Body") is the largest group, and is therefore the safest.

I confirm again that opponent and distractor words are unrelated to my group. The kill word "dresser" doesn't have a direct link to the remaining words.

Therefore, "Body" looks like the best bet.
CLUE: Body
TARGETS: teeth, kidney, elbow, chest


</thought>CLUE: BODY
TARGETS: teeth, kidney, elbow, chest
